summary: Martin Fowler introduces the concept of Language Workbenches, a term he coined in 2005 for tools that enable software development through multiple integrated domain-specific languages. These tools support Language-Oriented Programming by allowing developers to define DSL schemas, rich editing environments, and behavioral semantics. The key differentiator is their sophisticated editing environments that go beyond simple text editors, including projectional editors and illustrative programming approaches like spreadsheets. Fowler suggests these tools could significantly change the programming landscape, though they remain far from mainstream adoption.
key_points: Language workbenches enable Language-Oriented Programming by supporting multiple integrated DSLs for different areas of a system
The tools support three key capabilities: defining semantic model schemas, creating rich editing environments, and specifying behavioral semantics through interpretation or code generation
Editing environments are the distinguishing feature, ranging from text-based to projectional editors supporting structured text, diagrams, or both
Illustrative Programming weaves example execution into the editor, with spreadsheets being the most common example of this paradigm
money_quotes: They are one of the few things I feel could significantly change the programming landscape.

People have been making external DSLs for decades, but you edit these in a text editor. Language workbenches look to take this much further, approaching the level of modern PostIntelliJ IDEs, or even beyond.
When you edit a spreadsheet, the primary thing you see isn't the formulae that represents the behavioral definition, instead you see numbers that are calculated from the formulae.
This style of editing is startlingly different from our usual notions of a programming language, and could well be crucial to better engaging domain experts in software development.
The program and example data are woven together so as you edit the program you see the consequences immediately.
key_insight: Language workbenches differentiate themselves from traditional DSL tools through rich editing environments that could fundamentally change how domain experts engage with software development.
